title: Go Switch
content:
### Switch Statement

`switch` provides a cleaner alternative to long if-else chains.

### Basic Switch

```go
day := "Monday"
switch day {
case "Monday":
    fmt.Println("Start of work week")
case "Friday":
    fmt.Println("Almost weekend")
case "Saturday", "Sunday":
    fmt.Println("Weekend!")
default:
    fmt.Println("Regular day")
}
```

### No Break Needed

Go automatically breaks after each case. No `break` statement needed:

```go
switch x {
case 1:
    fmt.Println("one")
case 2:
    fmt.Println("two")
// No break - implicit
}
```

### Fallthrough

Use `fallthrough` to continue to the next case:

```go
switch num {
case 1:
    fmt.Println("one")
    fallthrough
case 2:
    fmt.Println("two") // also executed if num == 1
}
```

### Switch with No Expression

A switch without an expression is like `switch true`:

```go
score := 85
switch {
case score >= 90:
    grade = "A"
case score >= 80:
    grade = "B"
case score >= 70:
    grade = "C"
default:
    grade = "F"
}
```

### Type Switch

Switch on interface types:

```go
var val interface{} = 42
switch v := val.(type) {
case int:
    fmt.Println("Integer:", v)
case string:
    fmt.Println("String:", v)
default:
    fmt.Println("Unknown type")
}
```

> **Key Point:** Go switch is more flexible than C's - cases can have multiple values, no implicit fallthrough, and expressions are evaluated only once.

quiz.question: Does Go require break statements in switch cases?
Which keyword continues execution to the next case?
